title: Git Basics
description: Track your code changes, work safely in branches, push to GitHub, and automate tests with GitHub Actions.

steps.title: Why version control
steps.instruction:
## Tracking what changes

Every project eventually reaches the moment where you change something that worked and cannot remember what it looked like before. Version control solves this by saving snapshots of your code over time.

**Git** is the version control tool almost every developer uses. It runs on your machine and records the history of every change you make.

### What Git tracks

Git tracks a folder (your project) as a **repository**. It watches which files change, lets you group related changes into a **commit**, and keeps a log of every commit you ever made. You can always go back to any previous commit.

### Starting a repository

```bash
git init
```

Run this once inside your project folder. It creates a hidden `.git/` directory that stores the entire history. You never need to open that folder - Git manages it for you.

```bash
git status
```

Shows the current state: which files are new, which are modified, which are staged for the next commit.

---

**Your turn:** Initialise a Git repository in your project folder.
steps.starter_code:
# Run these commands in your project folder:

# Initialise a new repository
git init

# Check the current status
git status
steps.solution_code:
# Run these commands in your project folder:

# Initialise a new repository
git init

# Check the current status
git status
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Staging and committing
steps.instruction:
## Saving a snapshot

Git does not automatically save every change. You choose what goes into each snapshot - this is called **staging**.

The process is always:
1. Make changes to files
2. `git add` - stage the files you want to include
3. `git commit` - save the snapshot with a message

```bash
# Stage a single file
git add main.py

# Stage all changed files at once
git add .

# Save the snapshot
git commit -m "Add initial FastAPI app"
```

### Commit messages

Write your message in the present tense, describing what the commit does - not what you did:

```bash
# Good
git commit -m "Add user login endpoint"
git commit -m "Fix crash when todo text is empty"

# Avoid
git commit -m "changes"
git commit -m "fixed stuff"
```

### Viewing history

```bash
git log
```

Shows every commit: its ID (a long hash), who made it, when, and the message.

```bash
git log --oneline
```

Same list but compact - one line per commit.

---

**Your turn:** Stage all files and make your first commit.
steps.starter_code:
# Stage all files in the current folder
git add .

# Commit with a message
git commit -m "Initial commit"

# View the commit log
git log --oneline
steps.solution_code:
# Stage all files in the current folder
git add .

# Commit with a message
git commit -m "Initial commit"

# View the commit log
git log --oneline
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: What not to commit (.gitignore)
steps.instruction:
## Keeping secrets out of Git

Not everything in your project folder should go into Git:

- `.env` - contains API keys and passwords. If this ends up on GitHub, anyone can read your secrets.
- `venv/` - the virtual environment folder. It is large (hundreds of MB) and recreatable with `pip install`.
- `__pycache__/` - Python's compiled bytecode. Auto-generated, no value in version control.
- `*.db` - SQLite database files. These are runtime data, not source code.

### The .gitignore file

Create a file named `.gitignore` in your project root. Git reads it and skips anything matching the patterns inside:

```
venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.env
*.db
.DS_Store
```

Each line is a pattern. `*` means "any characters". A trailing `/` means it's a folder.

### Checking what Git sees

After creating `.gitignore`, run `git status` again. The ignored files should no longer appear in the list.

If you accidentally committed a secret before adding it to `.gitignore`, the file is now in your Git history. The safe response is to rotate the secret (generate a new one, invalidate the old one) - not just delete the file.

---

**Your turn:** Create a `.gitignore` that excludes your virtual environment, environment variables, compiled Python files, and SQLite databases.
steps.hint: The .gitignore content is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Branches
steps.instruction:
## Working safely with branches

A **branch** is a separate line of development. You can experiment on a branch without touching your working code. If the experiment works, you merge it in. If it doesn't, you discard the branch.

The default branch is called `main` (or `master` on older setups).

### Creating and switching branches

```bash
# Create a new branch and switch to it in one command
git checkout -b add-search-feature

# Modern equivalent (Git 2.23+)
git switch -c add-search-feature

# See all branches (the * marks the current one)
git branch
```

### Making commits on a branch

Any commits you make while on `add-search-feature` only exist on that branch. Your `main` branch is untouched.

### Merging back

When the feature is done, switch back to `main` and merge:

```bash
git checkout main
git merge add-search-feature
```

Git replays the branch's commits onto `main`. If both branches changed the same lines, Git will ask you to resolve the conflict manually.

### Deleting a merged branch

```bash
git branch -d add-search-feature
```

Cleans up after the merge. The commits are still there on `main` - the branch label is just removed.

---

**Your turn:** Create a branch, then merge it back into main.
steps.starter_code:
# Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b my-feature

# Make some changes, then commit them
git add .
git commit -m "Add my feature"

# Switch back to main
git checkout main

# Merge the feature branch in
git merge my-feature

# Clean up
git branch -d my-feature
steps.solution_code:
# Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b my-feature

# Make some changes, then commit them
git add .
git commit -m "Add my feature"

# Switch back to main
git checkout main

# Merge the feature branch in
git merge my-feature

# Clean up
git branch -d my-feature
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: GitHub - pushing your project
steps.instruction:
## Putting your code online

**GitHub** is a website that stores Git repositories. It gives you a backup, lets others see your code, and is where most open source projects live.

Your local repository and the GitHub repository are separate. You link them with a **remote** and **push** your commits up.

### One-time setup: create a repo on GitHub

1. Go to github.com and click **New repository**
2. Give it a name - no spaces, lowercase with hyphens works well
3. Do not initialise with a README (you already have local commits)
4. Copy the repository URL - it will look like `https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git`

### Linking and pushing

```bash
# Tell Git where the remote is (do this once)
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git

# Push your commits and set origin as the default remote
git push -u origin main
```

After the first push, future pushes are just:
```bash
git push
```

### Cloning an existing repo

If you want to download someone else's project (or your own on a new machine):

```bash
git clone https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git
```

This creates a folder with the full repository already inside.

---

**Your turn:** Link your local repository to GitHub and push your commits.
steps.starter_code:
# Add GitHub as the remote (replace with your actual URL)
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git

# Push all commits to GitHub
git push -u origin main

# From now on, just:
git push
steps.solution_code:
# Add GitHub as the remote (replace with your actual URL)
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git

# Push all commits to GitHub
git push -u origin main

# From now on, just:
git push
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: A working Git workflow
steps.instruction:
## The pattern that keeps projects clean

Now that you know the individual commands, here is the workflow that keeps your code organised as the project grows:

### Feature branch workflow

```bash
# 1. Start from a clean main
git checkout main
git pull                         # get latest changes if working with others

# 2. Create a branch for your task
git checkout -b add-auth

# 3. Do the work - commit often as you go
git add .
git commit -m "Add login endpoint"
git add .
git commit -m "Add token verification middleware"

# 4. When done, merge back
git checkout main
git merge add-auth
git push

# 5. Clean up
git branch -d add-auth
```

### Why this works

- `main` always has working code - never commit broken code directly to it
- Each branch is one task - easy to understand what changed and why
- Commits are small and frequent - easy to find when something broke
- The log becomes a history of decisions, not just a list of files changed

### Quick reference

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `git init` | Start a new repository |
| `git status` | See what has changed |
| `git add .` | Stage all changes |
| `git commit -m "msg"` | Save a snapshot |
| `git log --oneline` | View history |
| `git checkout -b name` | Create and switch to a branch |
| `git merge name` | Merge a branch into current |
| `git push` | Upload commits to GitHub |
| `git pull` | Download latest commits from GitHub |

---

**Your turn:** Write out the full feature branch workflow.
steps.starter_code:
# Full feature branch workflow

# 1. Start from main
git checkout main
git pull

# 2. Create a feature branch
git checkout -b my-feature

# 3. Work and commit
git add .
git commit -m "Implement feature"

# 4. Merge back to main and push
git checkout main
git merge my-feature
git push

# 5. Clean up
git branch -d my-feature
steps.solution_code:
# Full feature branch workflow

# 1. Start from main
git checkout main
git pull

# 2. Create a feature branch
git checkout -b my-feature

# 3. Work and commit
git add .
git commit -m "Implement feature"

# 4. Merge back to main and push
git checkout main
git merge my-feature
git push

# 5. Clean up
git branch -d my-feature
steps.hint: The workflow is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.

steps.title: CI with GitHub Actions
steps.instruction:
## Running your tests automatically

Right now you run `pytest` manually. **Continuous Integration (CI)** means your tests run automatically every time you push to GitHub. If tests pass, you can deploy with confidence. If they fail, GitHub shows you exactly which commit broke things before it reaches production.

**GitHub Actions** is GitHub's built-in CI system. It is free for public repositories and has a generous free tier for private ones.

### How it works

You create a YAML file inside `.github/workflows/`. GitHub reads it and runs the steps you define  -  on a fresh Linux machine, every time you push.

### A basic workflow

Create `.github/workflows/test.yml`:

```yaml
name: Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest
```

Breaking it down:
- `on: [push, pull_request]`  -  run on every push and on pull requests
- `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`  -  use a fresh Ubuntu machine
- `actions/checkout@v4`  -  clone your repo onto the machine
- `actions/setup-python@v5`  -  install the Python version you specify
- `run: pip install -r requirements.txt`  -  install your dependencies
- `run: pytest`  -  run your test suite

After you push this file, go to the **Actions** tab on your GitHub repo to watch it run. A green tick means tests passed. A red cross means something failed  -  click through to see which test and why.

---

**Your turn:** Write the GitHub Actions workflow file.
steps.starter_code:
name: Tests

# Run on every push and pull request
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest
steps.solution_code:
name: Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest
steps.hint: The workflow file is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.
